tweets.text: I do think there's potentially an "air pocket" risk here: as we transition from AI capex being predominantly financed by the two AI labs to being more broadly financed by the opex of hundreds to millions of enterprises. The largest enterprises are the slowest to adopt AI productively due to internal bureaucratic frictions and workflow complexities. The smallest companies will see the fastest AI adoption and the biggest ROI, but they are small. So from an aggregate ROI perspective, the total economic benefits of AI adoption may not get here fast enough to bridge the (perceived) gap of this transition from phase to another.

TBC, I don't agree with Dean, and I oppose his call for state intervention. But I'm explaining his argument, because most people refuse to actually engage with it.

He has a point that if Chinese frontier labs are now being encouraged to be totally open. China now sees this as explicitly part of their strategy. I would not assume that this is altruistic, but calculated, unlike the traditional OSS you lay out here.

If all of the Chinese labs are extremely unprofitable (they are), and they are encouraged at a state level to remain unprofitable, it is likely to have large and reverberating economic consequences on US AI as well.

That's Dean's point and I think it's worth taking seriously. I don't think China is encouraging this strategy with the same spirit of the people who built Linux.

https://t.co/u9TbCv95Jt

He has a point that if Chinese frontier labs are now being encouraged to be totally open. China now sees this as explicitly part of their strategy. I would not assume that this is altruistic, but calculated, unlike the traditional OSS you lay out here.

If all of the Chinese labs are extremely unprofitable (they are), and they are encouraged at a state level to remain unprofitable, it is likely to have large and reverberating economic consequences on US AI as well.

That's Dean's point and I think it's worth taking seriously. I don't think China is encouraging this strategy with the same spirit of the people who built Linux.

https://t.co/u9TbCv95Jt

No, I say it's to make money.

It's not the end of the world if that happens.

But it is a little creepy to have all your frontier models built by the government.

tweets.text:
Xi Jinping is not an OSS warrior. And yet he's now calling (as of 2 days ago) for open weight releases from Chinese labs.

Why?

Is he just altruistic? Is it a new Belt and Road initiative? Or does he want to compress private investment in the US Labs and push China to build a lead?

It's worth thinking about these questions seriously. I don't know the answer, but if you're not even thinking about the geopolitical questions here, you're missing the most important technological battle of the decade playing out right before your eyes.

Open source AI is great. It's got enormous positive externalities and as an investor and user of this stuff, it benefits me tremendously. I want it to continue, and I want the labs duopoly get eroded.

But there's a bigger game being played here. To China, OSS is not an ideology but a piece on that chess board.

tweets.text:
I agree with much of your response, but still some gaps

Tokens are powerful tools for pricing intangible goodwill in real time

Traditional accounts generally recognise goodwill during an acquisition, but tokens can continuously price the market’s belief in a creator brand

@RektBrands is a good example, with the token acting as a proxy for the goodwill @osf_rekt has built around the brand. My concern begins when this price discovery is presented as value accrual for holders w/o a defined mechanism connecting the two

i) HYPE + VALUE CAPTURE

I am not arguing that $ANSEM needs an equity wrapper. Crypto can create value through access, burns, buybacks, rewards, governance or mechanisms we have yet to discover, but holders still need to understand how value reaches them & who controls that process

HYPE sits beside a live protocol with observable users, fees & token purchases. $ANSEM’s value comes from separately owned businesses, creator fees, sponsorships & partnerships, with any benefit to holders remaining discretionary

ii) META AND BITCOIN

Instagram users are excluded from Meta’s financial upside, but giving them the opportunity to buy a token only introduces speculation unless that token has some defined connection to the value they create

Bitcoin’s collective belief is anchored to transparent issuance, permissionless settlement & protocol rules that no individual controls. $ANSEM remains dependent on Ansem deciding how supply, fees, partnerships & holder benefits are managed

iii) CREATOR GOODWILL

I agree that creators generate value that financial statements fail to capture. However, the example of teaching 100k people to make $10k cannot be reliably attributed or measured after accounting for losses, external information, capital + risk

This is where intangible value becomes dangerous. A creator can claim enormous economic impact while holders have no way to verify how much was created, who received it or whether any of it accrued to the token

iv) 58% SUPPLY

Holding 58% may reduce short-term selling & signal commitment, but it also creates overhang and dependence on one person’s decisions

Daily volume measures turnover rather than the liquidity available to absorb a controlling allocation. One airdrop having little impact does not establish how future distributions will behave at scale or in weaker markets

v) VALUE BEYOND PRICE

Your existing audience proves there is demand for content w/o token incentives. The missing step is showing how that activity becomes recurring value for holders

Access, education & partnerships could help, but holders need to know how these benefits are funded, allocated & connected to the token’s valuation

vi) WHY A TOKEN IS NEEDED

A token allows followers to benefit if its price rises, but that differs from sharing the upside generated by Ansem’s businesses or partnerships

The exploitation risk appears if the creator collects sponsorships & fees from accessing the community while holders provide liquidity, marketing & attention & carry the price risk

The audience already exists, but a tokenized network requires participants to create value for one another & a system that reliably distributes value back to them

vii) THE NEW PRIMITIVE

Experimentation is necessary, but removing traditional protections requires credible crypto-native replacements

If this model scales, every creator can issue a token, retain control, describe their influence as intangible value & promise that future activity may benefit holders. Genuine experimentation and deliberate exploitation will look identical while prices are rising

I remain open-minded about tokenized attention networks, but they need transparency

The honest framing today is a creator-backed memecoin acting as a real-time proxy for Ansem’s goodwill. That alone could make it highly successful

Leaving the value-accrual mechanism to be invented after the market has already priced the promise creates a lot of risk for the holder

#Uranium #Nuclear #Commodities

The long-term uranium price is already $97/lb.

Yet utility contracting still hasn't returned to normal replacement levels.

That raises an interesting question.

What happens when utilities need to secure years of uncovered fuel requirements?

Three observations make me think we will see much higher uranium prices this cycle.

1️⃣ The market may be tighter than it looks.

Cameco's Grant Isaac has repeatedly argued that uranium demand tends to be underestimated, while supply is often overestimated.

If that's true, and I believe it is, today's published deficits may understate the actual imbalance. I wouldn't be surprised if the below chart from Glodman Sachs soon will have to be revised again.

2️⃣ Prices have already moved—without a typical contracting cycle.

Spot uranium has risen from around $17/lb in 2016 to roughly $85/lb today.

The long-term price has climbed from $29/lb in 2018 to $97/lb today.

Yet utilities have not, as a group, returned to contracting at replacement-rate levels.

If they do, competition for available pounds could increase significantly.

3️⃣ Demand keeps expanding.

🇨🇳 China continues building reactors at an unprecedented pace.

🇮🇳 India is accelerating its nuclear build-out.

🌍 Many Western countries are extending reactor lives while planning new reactors.

Demand continues to grow.

New mine supply is responding much more slowly.

I'm curious how others see it.

Which part of this thesis do you agree, or disagree with?

👇 How to position? See my current highest-conviction uranium companies below.

tweets.text:
Everyone on my timeline is begging for the 2021 bull market to come back.

I’m not.

And that’s despite 2021 being the best year of my career.

I remember it vividly: projects raising nine figures without shipping a single line of code. Forks of forks valued at billions. Uniswap launches pumping 100x on day 1 with deep liquidity. Influencers charging six figures per tweet, dumping on their own communities the next morning.

Everything we touched turned to gold and everyone looked like a genius as the rising tide lifted all boats.

But deep down, we all knew none of it was real.

Don’t get me wrong, I’d be lying if I said I didn’t enjoy it. But 2021 is exactly why we are where we are today. We’ve spent the last few years paying off the excesses of that one crazy cycle.

Meanwhile, look at what’s actually happening right now:

Most of these people are gone. We have some trenchers left, but the underwhelming performance of recent memecoin pushes tells me they’re fading too. So many people left, but the investors and builders who stayed are the ones with high conviction who genuinely want this industry to succeed, and who actually contribute to it. And while CT declares this industry dead for the hundredth time, the biggest asset managers in the world are quietly accumulating at these levels, and governments themselves are calling for more pro-crypto clarity.

The bad news for you guys: the next cycle won’t feel like 2021. It will be much slower, much more boring, and way more institutional.

But mark my words: it will create more generational wealth than 2021 ever did.
And this time, it will flow to the people who actually deserve it and fought for this industry, not just those who happened to be in the right place at the right time.

The most exciting decade in crypto is just ahead of us.

tweets.text:
i'm in favor of rapid data center build out in the US

because most people don't realize it's, but it may be our last hope

we have no manufacturing left. and you can't wave a magic wand and bring it back. it will take much more deliberate and coherent effort then we're expending now, and it will take many decades. we're talking entire supply chains and speed to iterate through long-established colocation between suppliers, producers, and customers in Shenzen, etc.

most of our services economy, which is what replaced the old manufacturing economy, will get cannibalized by AI, and far more quickly than a lot of people expect

our financial sector depends on the primacy of the USD which is no longer a foregone conclusion

so what's the point of data centers then?

China can release open weight models, but they can't simply clone compute. they have to produce it, and right now they don't have access to all of the latest compute tech. but they are very, very resourceful and will quickly find ways to do more with less, so any current US advantage will not be insurmountable

but IF the US gets to self-improving AI which can generate novel research AND has the most compute AND gets a head start on robotics AND can get those robotics supply chains created domestically (some big ifs tbh), then the US still has a shot

if it doesn't do these things, then the US has possibly already lost. even worse, we're bleeding most of our soft power and global goodwill at the moment

i'm not being hyperbolic, but this may be our last stand. the vast majority of people just don't understand it yet

if business leaders and policy makers talked about it like this, and provided a vision of how it would benefit Americans, they'd likely find a lot more support around AI (study American support for various policies during the Cold War)

I've been cautious on crypto since we broke below $100k on BTC

I still think this current bounce is more of a short squeeze and we have one more retest/lower print in us

For this to be the bottom I would like to see a large pick-up in spot volume or a large increase in inflows

But I think the time to be bearish is now over.

I don't see BTC going materially below $60-65k from here

Saylor has bought himself a lot of time, institutional bottom targets are clustered around $50k, quantum threats still feel years away, expotential gains in AI drawing capital away seem behind us, bond yields hitting new highs globally will beget money printing, the 4 year cycle points to a Q4 rally...

The bearish trade has happened - waiting for some final flush is the wrong approach.

I still think its a patience game and the most obvious thing is probably just to set a DCA on BTC.

If you move too early down the risk curve and start buying alts you can still get crushed as it still feels PvP until new capital comes in.

But I'm looking exclusively at longs in crypto going forward with a 3-6 month time horizon.

tweets.text:
random story about Messi -

in 2016 I worked at Schibsted in Barcelona.

our office was in the same building as the Leo Messi Foundation (we shared one of the floors).

we had this glass wall meeting room where you could see straight into the lift lobby when the doors were open.

and one day Messi appeared!

two girls in our team were having a meeting with someone else joining on video conferencing on their laptop.

when they saw Messi in the lift lobby, they flipped out.

they turned the laptop around to show the rest on remote that GOAT was truly in their presence.

“it’s Messi!!”

I believe they literally ran out into the lift lobby - laptop in hand - in their excitement.

word spread fast and by the end of the day everyone had heard the story.

the next day, our office manager informed us a formal complaint had been submitted by the Foundation. we would have to keep the doors to the lift lobby shut from now on.

still annoyed I was not on that floor that day to see the GOAT in our office.

thankfully I did get to see him score on a freekick at Camp Nou at another occasion.

the greatest footballer to ever play the game.

tweets.text:
Most system prompts and skills have a very short life expectancy.

They are usually patches for the current model’s shortcomings. But those shortcomings are exactly what the next model gets trained and post-trained to fix…

An instruction that helps today may be unnecessary tomorrow, or worse, constrain a more capable model. IMO it’s best to reassess the entire system prompt and skills with each new model release. 

Same for skills. A skill explaining how to build a DCF, use an API, or follow a common workflow will eventually disappear because the model will know how to do it out of the box.

What survives is context that cannot live in the model weights: your preferences, your company’s private knowledge, your tools, your data, and the specific way you want work done.

The durable layer is not teaching the model general knowledge. It is teaching the model who you are.

tweets.text:
For the last 30 years, becoming an equity or fixed income analyst with a CFA was the best way to advance your career & have a premium salary in the investment industry.

Those skills will always be important to understand markets, but because AI is commoditizing a large portion of intelligence, the premium placed on those roles will go thru a 2nd derivative slowdown.

The increased premium will be on derivatives & market structure analysts.  

If AI allows us to do more with less, understanding who is swimming in your pool & what drives their decision making becomes increasingly important in public markets. 

This is similar to the NFL where we will now have to scout our opponents tendencies to get an edge and understand what’s driving a stock.

Access changes become agents for gamma squeezes. 

Politics become even more important to markets.

It’s the inevitable evolution of commoditization. 

What makes you think differently?!  

That’s where there will be a premium.

@jvisserlabs what do you think?!
